topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt like sandpaper under his fingertips. Elias stared at the meticulously typeset paragraphs, his breath catching in shallow, ragged gasps. Every sentence, every carefully chosen anecdote about his childhood, his triumphs, his supposed struggles—it was all a fabrication. He reread the final chapter, the part detailing his 'resilience,' and a hot flush crept up his neck, staining his cheeks crimson. His hands clenched into fists so tight his nails bit into his palms. A low, guttural sound vibrated in his chest, a pressure building behind his sternum that threatened to erupt. He slammed the document onto the mahogany desk, the sharp crack echoing loudly in the silent study. "This is a lie," he managed, his voice tight, strained to the breaking point. He stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the room, his movements jerky and uncontrolled, his gaze sweeping over the room as if searching for an escape route.

[story 2]

When Clara finally pieced together the discrepancies—the misplaced dates, the borrowed vocabulary, the sheer, audacious inaccuracy of the narrative—a cold, hard knot tightened in her stomach. She felt a sudden, intense heat radiating from her core, making her skin prickle. Her jaw locked so hard she could feel the muscles strain beneath her skin. She walked to the window, pressing her forehead against the cool glass, trying to regulate the frantic drumming of her pulse. The injustice of it fueled a simmering intensity. She turned back to the desk, her movements deliberate, each step heavy with suppressed force. She picked up a pen, not to write, but to grip it until her knuckles were white, then let it fall with a sharp clatter. "Who authorized this?" she whispered, the sound brittle, laced with a profound, vibrating disapproval.

[story 3]

Marcus sat motionless in the sterile conference room, the printed biography spread before him like an accusation. He didn't move for a long time, just absorbing the deceit. A deep, churning sensation rose in his gut, a physical churning that made his stomach clench painfully. He slowly pushed the papers away, the sound of the movement unnaturally loud in the quiet space. His breathing became shallow, rapid, each inhale a shallow gasp of frustration. He leaned back in his chair, the leather groaning beneath him, and slowly brought both hands up to grip the sides of his head, squeezing his temples until a blinding pressure built behind his eyes. "Unbelievable," he muttered, the word tasting like ash on his tongue, his voice dangerously low and vibrating with contained force.
